name: Symmetry Arguments
tagline: Exploit symmetry two ways: USE a hidden symmetry to skip case analysis, or BREAK an apparent symmetry to make a problem solvable.

blocks.text: Symmetry arguments come in two flavours. The first is using symmetry: when a problem has a structural symmetry, the answer often falls out by mapping one side to the other (e.g., constructing a bijection). The second is breaking symmetry: an apparently symmetric problem may have no solution UNLESS you introduce asymmetry by designating roles, picking a reference point, or fixing an orientation.

The Coin Piles problem (flip 20 coins to equalize heads) uses symmetry: the flipped subset and the unflipped rest become mirror images of each other in head-count. The Wise Men problem requires symmetry-breaking: 50 wise men can never coordinate counting unless one of them is designated as the spokesman.

blocks.label: Mental model
blocks.text: Always ask: 'Is there an exchange or relabeling that leaves the problem unchanged?' If yes, use it. If the problem demands an answer but is too symmetric to break the tie, ask: 'What asymmetric structure can I introduce that the problem permits?'

whenToUse: Counting problems where two configurations look swappable.
Existence proofs where you can pair items up in a symmetric way.
Combinatorial games or puzzles with a 'why would side A do better than side B?' structure.
Coordination problems where ALL agents are identical -- often the only solution is to assign roles.
recipe: Identify the symmetry: which transformation (swap, relabel, reflect, rotate) leaves the problem invariant?
If using symmetry: construct an explicit bijection between objects and their symmetric counterparts. The answer is often half of the total count, or follows from the count of fixed points.
If the problem feels deadlocked because everyone is the same, look for ways to break symmetry that the problem permits (designate a spokesman, fix the smallest index, choose an arbitrary axis).
Verify that the answer doesn't depend on which choice you made when breaking symmetry.
commonPitfalls: Assuming symmetry is exact when it's only approximate -- e.g., the two halves of a problem may look symmetric but differ in a detail that matters.
Failing to spot a hidden symmetry because it's encoded in an unusual way (e.g., 'all bags are mislabeled' is a cyclic-permutation symmetry).
Trying to use symmetry to find an answer when the problem actually requires breaking it.
Breaking symmetry in a way that biases the answer (the choice should be arbitrary; if it matters, you broke the wrong symmetry).
